message.content:
Synthesise how the SOTA deep-research systems actually beat the human reference on DeepResearch Bench, and what POLARIS can steal.

POLARIS's MEASURED position on DeepResearch Bench task 72 (RACE, gpt-5.5 evaluator, k=5 pinned):
  overall 0.4382 | comprehensiveness 0.4549 | insight 0.4238 | instruction-following 0.4409 | readability 0.3774
  baseline was 0.4062. Human reference = 0.5000 by construction. SOTA (ADORE) = 0.5265. Our gap = 0.088.
  RACE formula: Overall = target/(target+reference), an LLM judge scores BOTH against per-task weighted criteria.
  Task-72 dimension weights: insight 0.32, comprehensiveness 0.29, instruction-following 0.25, readability 0.14.
  KEY MECHANIC WE VERIFIED: RACE runs an LLM "ArticleCleaner" that STRIPS ALL CITATION MARKERS, REFERENCE LISTS
  AND FOOTNOTES before the judge reads anything (our 9,300 words -> 7,692; 345 [n] markers -> 0; bibliography deleted).
  So citation/bibliography quality is INVISIBLE to RACE. Only the running prose scores.
  OUR KNOWN WEAKNESS: the judge reads 12 paragraphs averaging 633 words, 0 subsections, 0 tables, 0 bullets.
  The human reference: 59 paragraphs averaging 142 words, 24 H3, 10 tables, 91 bullets. We LIST findings; the
  reference EXPLAINS them. Our pipeline span-grounds every sentence, which structurally forbids cross-source
  inference — the sentence type that earns INSIGHT (0.32, heaviest weight, our worst dimension).

CONFIRMED FINDINGS (these survived adversarial verification against primary sources):
[
 {
  "system": "ADORE",
  "claim": "ADORE is NOT SOTA on DeepResearch Bench, and 52.65 is not the number to beat. ADORE does not appear on EITHER official leaderboard (legacy Gemini-2.5-Pro or current GPT-5.5). Its '#1' claim was true only against a ~Jan-2026 leaderboard snapshot that is now badly stale.",
  "detail": "I pulled the leaderboard's actual source-of-truth CSVs from the HF Space repo (not the rendered page). ADORE's paper Table 2 lists its competitors as Tavily 52.44, ThinkDepthAI 52.43, CellCog 51.94, Salesforce Air 50.65, LangChain(GPT-5) 50.60 — those rows match the LEGACY leaderboard exactly (tavily-research 52.44, thinkdepthai 52.43, salesforce-air 50.65, gensee-search-gpt-5 50.60), confirming ADORE benchmarked against a legacy-judge snapshot. But on the CURRENT legacy board, 21 systems now sit ABOVE 52.65 (qianfan 58.03, ZTE-Nebula 57.27, Link 57.08, zhipu 57.06, xiaoyi 57.00, WhaleCloud 56.81, cellcog-max 56.67, 1688AILab 56.53, octen 56.31, grep-v5 56.23, nvidia-aiq 55.95, ... RecallRadar 53.19). Note CellCog was 51.94 in ADORE's snapshot and is 56.67 today — the board moved ~4-5 pts under ADORE. ADORE would rank ~#22 today. Do not plan against 0.5265 as 'SOTA'; this is exactly the 48.88 mistake repeating.",
  "url": "https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/raw/main/data/leaderboard.csv"
 },
 {
  "system": "other",
  "claim": "The real SOTA under POLARIS's own judge (GPT-5.5) is cellcog-max at 55.78, not 52.65. POLARIS's true gap is ~0.120, not 0.088.",
  "detail": "The benchmark switched its official RACE evaluator from Gemini-2.5-Pro to GPT-5.5 on 11 May 2026 (Gemini-2.5-Pro deprecation), and maintains a SEPARATE data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv. Full GPT-5.5 board: cellcog-max 55.78 (comp 56.34 / insight 57.08 / IF 55.30 / read 51.94), WhaleCloud-DocChain 54.78, bodhi 54.07, lunon 53.51, dalpha 53.10, sourcery 51.17, gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch 49.98, openai-deepresearch 47.84, perplexity 43.05, grok-deeper-search 41.22. Since POLARIS is measured with the gpt-5.5 evaluator, this is the ONLY directly comparable board. Cross-judge scores are NOT comparable and the mapping is nonlinear (compresses the top, lifts the bottom): cellcog 56.67→55.78, gemini-DR 49.71→49.98, openai-DR 46.45→47.84, perplexity 40.46→43.05, grok 38.22→41.22. Target 55.78.",
  "url": "https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/raw/main/data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv"
 },
 {
  "system": "RACE-judge-itself",
  "claim": "RACE's ArticleCleaner strips ONLY citations/references/footnotes and is explicitly instructed to preserve everything else — so markdown tables, H2/H3 headers and bullets DO survive to the judge. POLARIS's plan to add structure is safe.",
  "detail": "The actual cleaning prompt (prompt/clean_prompt.py): 'Cleaning goal: remove all citation links, citation marks (such as [1], [2]...), reference lists, footnotes, and ensure the content reads smoothly. KEEP ALL OTHER ORIGINAL CONTENT; REMOVE ONLY CITATIONS.' Special case: a chunk that is entirely a bibliography returns empty string. Note pipeline v2 (May 2026) made cleaning CHUNK-BASED for long articles ('using a chunk-based strategy to better support very long articles'), operating per-fragment with no cross-chunk context — a minor risk that a table straddling a chunk boundary gets mangled, but headers/bullets/tables are not targets of removal. This confirms POLARIS's verified finding (citations invisible) AND extends it: structure is NOT invisible, it is fully visible.",
  "url": "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench/main/prompt/clean_prompt.py"
 },
 {
  "system": "RACE-judge-itself",
  "claim": "RACE is a PAIRWISE side-by-side judgment in a single prompt, and the target is ALWAYS article_1 while the human reference is ALWAYS article_2 — there is no position randomization or order counterbalancing.",
  "detail": "deepresearch_bench_race.py lines 101-102 pass `article_1=target_article, article_2=reference_article` with no shuffle/swap anywhere. The score prompt puts BOTH full articles in one context and instructs: 'Please strictly evaluate and compare <article_1> and <article_2> based on EACH criterion... 2. Comparative Evaluation: Analyze how the two articles perform on each criterion... 3. Score Separately: score each article on each criterion (0-10 continuous).' Anchors: 0-2 very poor, 2-4 poor, 4-6 average, 6-8 good, 8-10 excellent. Consequence: this is a CONTRAST judgment, not an absolute one. The judge writes a comparative 'analysis' field BEFORE emitting scores for each criterion, so a structurally impoverished article (0 subsections, 0 tables, 0 bullets next to a reference with 24 H3s, 10 tables, 91 bullets) gets that contrast narrated into the analysis text on EVERY criterion — including insight criteria — and drags scores that are nominally about analysis depth. Position is fixed, so it is a constant across all systems (not an exploitable lever, but it means measured deltas aren't position noise).",
  "url": "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench/main/deepresearch_bench_race.py"
 },
 {
  "system": "RACE-judge-itself",
  "claim": "Confirmed aggregation: overall = target_total/(target_total+reference_total), and EACH dimension is independently normalized as target_dim/(target_dim+ref_dim). Both are weighted sums of per-criterion 0-10 scores using LLM-generated per-task criterion weights.",
  "detail": "utils/score_calculator.py + deepresearch_bench_race.py lines 155-170: 'overall_score = target_total / (target_total + reference_total)' and 'normalized_dims[dim] = target_score / (target_score + reference_score)'. Weighted scores = sum over criteria of (criterion_weight * raw 0-10 score), then dimension_weight applied. Practical implication for POLARIS: insight 0.4238 means target_insight ≈ 0.735 × reference_insight. If the human reference scores ~8/10 on insight criteria, POLARIS is scoring ~5.9/10 — i.e. sitting in the '4-6 = average, basically meets the criterion, neither good nor bad' band. Getting to parity (0.50) requires moving insight criteria from the 'average' band to the 'good' (6-8) band, not a wholesale rewrite.",
  "url": "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench/main/utils/score_calculator.py"
 },
 {
  "system": "RACE-judge-itself",
  "claim": "The judge's INSIGHT rubric explicitly rewards exactly the sentence types POLARIS's span-grounding forbids: causal mechanisms, second-order/non-obvious effects, cross-source synthesis, challenging conventional wisdom, and forward-looking scenarios. 'Information listing' is explicitly named as the anti-pattern.",
  "detail": "criteria_prompt_en.py, generate_eval_criteria_prompt_insight: 'Beyond Surface-Level: Criteria should assess analytical depth, logical rigor, originality of insights, and value of conclusions, NOT JUST INFORMATION LISTING.' The canonical exemplar rubric it few-shots the judge with: (1) 'Depth of Analysis of Core Drivers and Mechanisms' 0.20 — 'deeply analyzes their INTERPLAY AND CAUSAL MECHANISMS, rather than a superficial listing'; (2) 'Sophistication in Uncovering Market Impacts' 0.20 — 'goes beyond obvious impacts to uncover SUBTLE OR SECOND-ORDER EFFECTS'; (3) 'Logical Coherence and Justification' 0.20 — recommendations 'logically DERIVED FROM the impact analysis'; (4) 'Nuance in Risk-Return Analysis' 0.15; (5) 'Originality and Strategic Value' 0.15 — 'unique perspectives, CHALLENGES CONVENTIONAL WISDOM... beyond common knowledge'; (6) 'Forward-Looking Perspective' 0.10. Every one of these six is a cross-source inference or a claim that no single span supports. A pipeline that span-grounds every sentence is structurally incapable of scoring above the 4-6 'average' band here. This is the mechanism behind insight being POLARIS's worst dimension at its heaviest weight (0.32).",
  "url": "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench/main/prompt/criteria_prompt_en.py"
 },
 {
  "system": "RACE-judge-itself",
  "claim": "The judge's READABILITY rubric explicitly scores heading levels and the presence of TABLES/CHARTS as a named criterion. POLARIS's readability 0.3774 is below EVERY system on the GPT-5.5 leaderboard (worst listed: grok at 45.72) — it is POLARIS's single most anomalous number and the cheapest to fix.",
  "detail": "criteria_prompt_en.py readability exemplar criteria: 'Overall Article Structure and Logical Flow' (weight 0.25) — 'clear logical structure... with DISTINCT HEADING LEVELS, effectively guiding the reader'; and 'Information Presentation' — 'Is key information highlighted? Is data presented clearly, WITH APPROPRIATE USE OF CHARTS AND TABLES to illustrate trends and comparisons?'; plus 'Paragraph Cohesion' (0.15) — 'each paragraph focuses on a SINGLE IDEA'. POLARIS's 12 paragraphs × 633 words with 0 H3 / 0 tables / 0 bullets fails three named criteria simultaneously and mechanically. Even though readability carries the lowest task-72 weight (0.14), a 0.377→0.50 move is worth ~+0.017 overall on its own — and because the judge is pairwise/contrastive (see other finding), the structural deficit also bleeds into the insight and comprehensiveness analyses.",
  "url": "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench/main/prompt/criteria_prompt_en.py"
 },
 {
  "system": "ADORE",
  "claim": "ADORE's actual method is a 5-agent hub-and-spoke pipeline whose central idea — 'memory-locked synthesis' — is a HARDER span-grounding constraint than POLARIS already has. ADORE is therefore the wrong system to copy for an insight problem; its own paper contains no ablations and does not name its LLM.",
  "detail": "ADORE = Atlassian (You, Sun, Bora, Li, Goel, Li, Culatana; arXiv 2601.18267, 26 Jan 2026, SIGIR'26 submission). Agents: Orchestrator (routes fast-RAG vs deep-research by 'research complexity'); Grounding (clarification questions to fix underspecified prompts); Planning (sections/priorities/success criteria as a user-editable shared artifact); Execution (Self-Evolution Engine evolves queries from detected evidence gaps); Report Generation (initial outline → outline revision on retrieved evidence → final report); WebSearch. Three contributions: (1) Memory-locked synthesis — 'each section is written using ONLY the section-scoped evidence stored in the claim–evidence graph... traceability by construction'; (2) Evidence-coverage-guided execution — section-level coverage audits drive targeted re-retrieval, stopping on coverage+citation-stability rather than a fixed iteration budget; (3) Section-packed long-context grounding — pack only section-relevant evidence per generation call, prune low-salience text, compress sources into citation-preserving summaries. CRITICAL READ: (1) is a STRICTER admissible-evidence constraint than POLARIS's span-grounding, and RACE strips citations anyway, so ADORE's headline contribution buys POLARIS nothing on RACE. Its real, transferable content is (2)+(3), which are re-derivations of WebWeaver. The paper has NO ablation table, names no backbone model, and its RACE numbers (52.65 / comp 52.22 / insight 54.37 / IF 51.11 / read 52.18) are self-reported under the legacy judge.",
  "url": "https://arxiv.org/html/2601.18267v1"
 },
 {
  "system": "other",
  "claim": "Concrete shape of a winning report, measured: ~26,000 output tokens written in ~25 DISCRETE writing steps (i.e. ~25 sections, ~1k tokens each), from a ~4,900-token outline refined over ~2.2 optimization rounds, backed by ~112 saved pages / ~67k evidence tokens gathered over ~15.7 search steps and ~20 unique queries.",
  "detail": "WebWeaver Table 3 (Claude-Sonnet-4 on DeepResearch Bench): #search steps 15.71, #outline tokens 4876.21, #outline optimizations 2.16, #saved pages 112.25, #search queries 20.24, #evidence tokens 67,237, #summary tokens 14,980, #output tokens 26,127, #writing steps 24.78. Paper's gloss: 'the writer's process of composing a 26k-token report in 25 discrete writing steps validates that our hierarchical synthesis is a practical way to maintain coherence over long outputs.' Compare POLARIS: 12 paragraphs, one monolithic composition pass. The human reference (59 paragraphs, 24 H3, 10 tables, 91 bullets) is structurally much closer to the 25-section WebWeaver shape than to POLARIS's. The '~25 writing steps' is the number to engineer toward — one LLM call per H3.",
  "url": "https://arxiv.org/html/2509.13312v3"
 },
 {
  "system": "other",
  "claim": "A third, distinct composition paradigm worth knowing: TTD-DR (Google) writes a deliberately BAD full draft first from parametric knowledge, then 'denoises' it with retrieval — the evolving draft steers the next search query. Plus component-wise self-evolution with an LLM-judge fitness score on every stage.",
  "detail": "TTD-DR (arXiv 2507.16075, Google). Report generation as a diffusion process: 'we prompt an LLM to generate an initial draft report based on the user's query. This draft serves as a NOISY starting point.' Algorithm 1: feed current draft into the search-question generator → retrieve → synthesize answer → 'the new information is used to revise the report draft, either by adding new details or by verifying existing information' → loop. Explicitly contrasted with per-section search: 'TTD-DR AVOIDS SEPARATED SEARCHES FOR EACH SECTION TO MAINTAIN GLOBAL CONTEXT.' Second mechanism, Self-Evolution: each stage agent (plan, question, answer, report) spawns multiple variants, each scored by an LLM-as-a-judge auto-rater on Helpfulness/Comprehensiveness which returns BOTH a fitness score and a textual critique, variants are revised on the critique, then merged. Reported: 74.5% win rate vs OpenAI DR on long-form. NOTE: TTD-DR reports DeepConsult/HLE/GAIA — I did NOT find a RACE score for it, so it is not directly rankable here. Its self-evolution loop (generate variants → LLM-judge critique → revise → merge) is the most direct known way to optimize prose AGAINST a rubric, which is what POLARIS needs for the insight criteria.",
  "url": "https://arxiv.org/html/2507.16075v1"
 },
 {
  "system": "RACE-judge-itself",
  "claim": "Cross-judge scores are not comparable, and the benchmark now also has a successor (DRB II). Any number quoted for a system without naming its evaluator is untrustworthy — this is the trap that produced both the 48.88 and the 52.65 errors.",
  "detail": "Repo news, 11 May 2026: official evaluator switched Gemini-2.5-Pro → GPT-5.5 (FACT pipeline → GPT-5.4-mini), chosen by alignment with human judgments on a 50-task × 4-DRA annotated subset against a human inter-annotator agreement baseline of 68.78% (GPT-5.5 71.82 overall / PAR 73.00 / OPC 89.70 / FAP 65.35 / FAS 59.23, beating Gemini-3.1-Pro 70.58 and Claude-Opus-4-7 70.11). Two leaderboards were maintained in parallel during a dual-acceptance window to 31 May 2026; after 1 June only the GPT-5.5 board is maintained. The 48.88 figure POLARIS was previously burned by is the STATIC table still sitting on deepresearch-bench.github.io (gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch, old judge) — that page is stale and should be treated as poisoned. ADORE's 52.65 is the same class of error one generation later. Also note DeepResearch Bench II shipped 6 Feb 2026 (arXiv 2601.08536) with a different evaluation focus, though DRB continues to be maintained.",
  "url": "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench/main/README.md"
 },
 {
  "system": "Tavily",
  "claim": "Tavily Research's report-composition architecture is a FOUR-STAGE pipeline with exactly ONE report-writing step: Planning -> WebSearch -> ResearchSubtopic (pro only) -> Generating. There is no section-by-section writer, no separate outline agent, and no self-critique/revision pass exposed anywhere.",
  "detail": "Tavily's own docs enumerate every tool the agent can emit during a run. Verbatim from the streaming reference: `Planning` = 'Initializes the research plan based on the input query' (both models); `WebSearch` = 'Executes web searches to gather information' (both); `ResearchSubtopic` = 'Conducts deep research on specific subtopics' (Pro only); `Generating` = 'Generates the final research report from collected information' (both). The documented 'Research Flow Example' is: Planning tool_call/response -> WebSearch tool_call (with `queries` array) / tool_response (with `sources` array) -> (Pro mode) ResearchSubtopic tool_call/response cycles -> Generating tool_call ('Generating final report') -> Generating tool_response ('Report generated') -> Content events (streamed report chunks) -> Sources event -> Done. This identical 4-tool table is independently reproduced in Tavily's own open-source skills repo (github.com/tavily-ai/skills, skills/tavily-best-practices/references/research.md, lines 182-193), which is a second Tavily-authored source. Note: absence of a critique/revision tool is absence-of-evidence (internals are closed), but two independent Tavily-authored sources enumerate the full tool set and neither contains one.",
  "url": "https://docs.tavily.com/documentation/api-reference/endpoint/research-streaming"
 },
 {
  "system": "other",
  "claim": "CORRECTION, HIGH PRIORITY: Tavily is NOT SOTA and ADORE (0.5265) is NOT the SOTA target. On the official DeepResearch Bench leaderboard, tavily-research is 52.44 and ranks 23rd of 45. The real leader is 58.03 (Gemini-2.5-Pro judge) / 55.78 (GPT-5.5 judge). 'ADORE' does not appear on either leaderboard at all.",
  "detail": "I pulled the live leaderboard CSVs directly from the benchmark's HuggingFace space (muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard, lastModified 2026-06-28), which is the maintained successor to the stale deepresearch-bench.github.io page (that page still shows the 48.88 Gemini number you got burned by). Two CSVs exist because the benchmark switched judges on 11 May 2026. GEMINI-2.5-PRO JUDGE (data/leaderboard.csv, 45 systems): qianfan_deepresearch_0430 58.03 | ZTE-Nebula 57.27 | Link 57.08 | zhipu 57.06 | xiaoyi 57.00 | ... | cellcog-max 56.67 | ... | tavily-research 52.44 (comp 52.84, insight 53.59, IF 51.92, read 49.21) | ... | gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch 49.71 | openai-deepresearch 46.45 | langchain-open-deep-research 43.44 | perplexity 40.46 | grok 38.22. GPT-5.5 JUDGE (data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv, only 10 systems re-scored so far -- and THIS is the judge POLARIS is pinned to): cellcog-max 55.78 (comp 56.34, insight 57.08, IF 55.30, read 51.94) | WhaleCloud-DocChain 54.78 | bodhi 54.07 | lunon 53.51 | dalpha 53.10 | sourcery 51.17 | gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch 49.98 | openai-deepresearch 47.84 | perplexity 43.05 | grok 41.22. Tavily has NOT been re-scored under GPT-5.5. Implication: POLARIS at 0.4382 under the GPT-5.5 judge is comparable ONLY to that second list; the true gap to SOTA is ~0.12 (to cellcog-max 0.5578), not 0.088. Tavily's blog headline 'SOTA on DeepResearch Bench' is not supported by the leaderboard -- it was likely true only against a narrow comparison set (they benchmark themselves against LangChain's Open Deep Research, 43.44). Do NOT plan against Tavily as the ceiling. Local copies: /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/leaderboard_gemini25pro_judge.csv and leaderboard_gpt55_judge.csv.",
  "url": "https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/resolve/main/data/leaderboard.csv"
 },
 {
  "system": "Tavily",
  "claim": "MEASURED on task 72 (the exact task POLARIS is tuning): Tavily's report is 6,007 words / 1 H1 / 8 H2 / 28 H3 / 98 paragraphs averaging 20.2 words / 152 bullets / 0 tables. Its 'paragraphs' are 20-word fragments interleaved with bullet lists -- it is a structured brief, not prose.",
  "detail": "Tavily's task-72 outline is 8 numbered H2 sections each split into 3-6 H3 subsections: 1. Introduction: AI as Core Driver of 4IR / 2. Core Theoretical Frameworks (2.1 Task-Based Models 2.2 Skill-Biased & Routine-Biased Change 2.3 Superstar Firms 2.4 Platformization 2.5 Organizational Complementarities) / 3. Mechanisms of Labour Market Restructuring (3.1-3.6) / 4. Heterogeneous Impacts (4.1 Skill Level 4.2 Occupation Type 4.3 Gender 4.4 Age 4.5 Geography) / 5. Industry-Specific Restructuring (5.1-5.6) / 6. Equity, Ethics, Policy (6.1-6.3) / 7. Areas of Consensus and Remaining Uncertainties (7.1 Broad Consensus 7.2 Uncertainties and Debates) / 8. Conclusion. Note sections 7.1/7.2 -- an explicit consensus-vs-open-questions section is the standard insight-farming move and appears in every top report. Across its 46 English reports Tavily averages 4,749 words, 13.2 H3+, 28.1 bullets/1k words, 0.2 table rows. Directly relevant to POLARIS: Tavily's readability (49.21) is its WORST dimension and its lowest rank-relative score -- it is the most bullet-heavy system I measured, and it is punished for it.",
  "url": "https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/resolve/main/data/raw_data/tavily-research.jsonl"
 },
 {
  "system": "Tavily",
  "claim": "Tavily's headline engineering claim is about COST, not report quality: 66% fewer tokens than LangChain Open Deep Research, by making tool-caller context grow linearly O(n+m) instead of quadratically O(n*m), saving a factor of (m+1)/2 per agent.",
  "detail": "Verbatim: 'Through this methodology, we were able to reduce token consumption by 66% (when compared to Open Deep Research) while achieving SOTA on DeepResearch Bench.' The mechanism: in a standard ReAct loop 'tool calls and outputs are propagated through the tool calling loop, with previously retrieved/generated tokens being persisted in the context window on each subsequent iteration' -- quadratic. Tavily replaces persisted tool output with 'knowledge distillations, [which] even when aggregated, are negligible when compared to the quantity of tokens gathered from web' -- linear. n = tokens per tool-calling invocation, m = number of tool-calling iterations. Also relevant: they explicitly DISCLAIM benchmark-chasing -- 'Rather than optimizing for benchmark scores, we optimized for directional feedback... the best outcome is rarely the highest numerical score. For production systems, improvements like reduced token usage, reliability, lower latency, and fewer failures are more valuable than a one-point bump on an eval.' They also say they threw away their first, more 'complicated and sophisticated' deep-research architecture entirely ('we had to abandon our first attempt at deep research and rebuild the entire system from scratch') in favor of 'simplify orchestration logic and lean into autonomy'. TAKEAWAY: Tavily is a search-infrastructure company optimizing for API cost per call; they are NOT the right role model for maximizing RACE. The systems at the top of the board write 2-9x more words than Tavily and clearly spend the tokens.",
  "url": "https://www.tavily.com/blog/research-en"
 },
 {
  "system": "Tavily",
  "claim": "Tavily's 'Pro' mode is multi-agent SUBTOPIC RESEARCH (parallel/iterative ResearchSubtopic cycles), not multi-agent WRITING. The subtopic decomposition happens in Planning and drives retrieval; a single writer then composes the whole report.",
  "detail": "API reference verbatim on the `model` param: 'mini is optimized for targeted, efficient research and works best for narrow or well-scoped questions. pro provides comprehensive, multi-angle research and is suited for complex topics that span multiple subtopics or domains' (options: mini, pro, auto; default auto). Best-practices doc: pro = 'Comprehensive, multi-agent research for complex, multi-domain topics'. The ONLY Pro-exclusive tool in the streamed tool table is `ResearchSubtopic` ('Conducts deep research on specific subtopics') -- a retrieval tool. `Generating` is listed as 'Both', i.e. mini and pro share the identical single report-generation step. Other output knobs, all verbatim: `output_length` enum {short, standard, long}, default standard -- 'Typed control over response size. Ranges are targets, not hard caps -- individual responses can fall outside when the question requires it' (NO word counts published); `citation_format` enum {numbered, mla, apa, chicago}, default numbered; `output_schema` -- a JSON Schema that forces structured JSON output instead of a markdown report; `include_domains` ('Soft preference for sources', max 20) / `exclude_domains` ('Hard blocklist', max 20); `files` (up to 5 text/md/json, max 80,000 words each). Note that citation_format is IRRELEVANT under RACE, since RACE's ArticleCleaner strips all citation markers before judging -- consistent with what POLARIS already verified.",
  "url": "https://docs.tavily.com/documentation/api-reference/endpoint/research"
 },
 {
  "system": "other",
  "claim": "TRAP TO AVOID: web search results attribute a detailed 'Report Structure Planner / Section Formatter / Section Knowledge Agent / Query Generator / Result Accumulator / Reflection Agent / Final Section Formatter / Finalizer' pipeline to Tavily. This is NOT Tavily's architecture -- it is a third-party Medium/TDS tutorial that merely USES the Tavily search API.",
  "detail": "The search index blends the Tavily docs with Robert Martin-Short's 'Building a Research Agent That Can Write to Google Docs' (Towards Data Science) and a LangGraph+Tavily Medium tutorial, and the snippet-summarizer presents that tutorial's LangGraph node names as if they were Tavily Research's internal stages. I verified against the raw Tavily docs HTML and Tavily's own skills repo: the ONLY tools Tavily Research emits are Planning, WebSearch, ResearchSubtopic, Generating. There is no 'Reflection Agent' node and no per-section formatter in Tavily Research. If anyone on the team cites a Tavily 'Section Formatter' or 'Reflection Agent', it is contamination from a tutorial.",
  "url": "https://towardsdatascience.com/building-a-research-agent-that-can-write-to-google-docs-part-1-4b49ea05a292/"
 },
 {
  "system": "other",
  "claim": "CONVERGENT EVIDENCE for one-shot writing: a separate arXiv deep-research paper independently argues for 'One Shot Report Generation' over a unified global context, on the grounds that parallel/section-siloed writing produces 'siloed knowledge' and loses narrative unity.",
  "detail": "'Deep Researcher with Sequential Plan Reflection and Candidates Crossover' (arXiv 2601.20843) abstract, verbatim: 'The sequential refinement process ... allows the agent to maintain a centralized Global Research Context, enabling it to look back at current progress, reason about the research plan, and intelligently make changes at runtime. This dynamic adaptation contrasts with parallel approaches, which often suffer from siloed knowledge. ... The process concludes with One Shot Report Generation, ensuring the final document is informed by a unified narrative and high fact density.' They report 46.21 overall on DeepResearch Bench with Gemini-2.5-Pro, so this is NOT a strong system and the claim should not be over-weighted -- but it converges with Tavily's independently-arrived-at design (raw sources re-injected once, single Generating step) and with the observation that the top system's insight comes from paragraphs that reconcile sources ACROSS sections. - deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

import json, re

def measure(article):
    lines = article.split('\n')
    h2 = sum(1 for l in lines if re.match(r'^##\s', l))
    h3 = sum(1 for l in lines if re.match(r'^###\s', l))
    bullets = sum(1 for l in lines if re.match(r'^\s*[-*+]\s', l) or re.match(r'^\s*\d+\.\s', l))
    table_rows = sum(1 for l in lines if l.strip().startswith('|'))
    tables = 0
    in_t = False
    for l in lines:
        if l.strip().startswith('|'):
            if not in_t: tables += 1; in_t = True
        else: in_t = False
    words = len(article.split())
    # paragraphs: non-empty, non-header, non-bullet, non-table blocks
    paras = [p for p in re.split(r'\n\s*\n', article) if p.strip() and not re.match(r'^\s*(#|\||[-*+]\s|\d+\.\s)', p.strip())]
    pw = [len(p.split()) for p in paras]
    avgpw = sum(pw)/len(pw) if pw else 0
    return dict(words=words, h2=h2, h3=h3, bullets=bullets, tables=tables, table_rows=table_rows, paras=len(paras), avg_para_words=round(avgpw,1))

for f in ['cellcog.jsonl','onyx.jsonl','baidu-qianfan-drs-pro.jsonl','deepinsight.jsonl','tavily.jsonl']:
    for line in open(f):
        d = json.loads(line)
        if d.get('id') == 72:
            art = d['article']
            print(f, measure(art))
            # print prompt snippet
            print('  prompt:', d.get('prompt','')[:120])
            break
EOF

content.content:
### 8.2 Outstanding Tensions and Unresolved Questions

Several important tensions in the literature remain unresolved. The most fundamental is the gap between task-level productivity evidence and aggregate employment outcomes. The micro-level experiments consistently show large productivity gains; the macro-level evidence shows modest or null employment effects. This could mean that AI's labor market impact is still early and will intensify; that productivity gains are being absorbed through shorter hours, task expansion, or organizational slack rather than headcount reduction; or that general equilibrium effects — new demand, new products, new tasks — are already offsetting displacement. The current evidence cannot definitively distinguish among these explanations.

A second tension concerns the temporal horizon. Most of the evidence reviewed here covers at most two to three years of post-generative AI experience. Historical analogies suggest that the full labor market effects of general-purpose technologies can take decades to materialize. The current evidence may therefore capture only the earliest phase of a much longer adjustment process — a concern reinforced by the productivity paradox literature, which documents repeated instances of technologies showing limited initial impact before producing transformative effects once complementary organizational and institutional changes are in place.

A third tension lies between the employment quantity and employment quality perspectives. The dominant task-based framework, which has driven most empirical research, focuses on how many tasks are automated and how many jobs are created or destroyed. The labor process perspective, supported by a growing but still smaller literature, suggests that employment quality may be deteriorating in ways that task-level analyses do not capture — through algorithmic surveillance, reduced autonomy, deskilling through augmentation, and intensified managerial control. A complete assessment of AI's labor market impact requires both perspectives, yet they are rarely integrated within the same study.


=====

### 7.1 The Denmark-U.S. Divergence: Institutions as the Key Variable

The divergence between the Danish and U.S. evidence on generative AI's employment effects is among the most consequential empirical findings in the recent literature. Humlum and Vestergaard (2025) found precise null effects on earnings and hours in Denmark; Brynjolfsson, Chandar, and Chen (2025) found a 16% relative employment decline for young workers in AI-exposed occupations in the United States. Both studies use rigorous methods and credible data. The divergence is therefore most plausibly attributable to institutional context rather than methodology.

Several institutional features distinguish the Danish from the American labor market. First, Denmark's flexicurity model combines relatively easy hiring and firing with generous unemployment insurance and extensive active labor market programs, enabling restructuring to proceed through smooth worker transitions rather than through reduced hiring and informal exclusion. Second, Denmark's union density — approximately 67–70% — ensures that AI adoption decisions are more likely to be negotiated, with bargaining over retraining commitments, transition periods, and task reallocation rather than unilateral firm-level deployment. Third, Denmark's adult learning infrastructure is among the most developed in the OECD, with continuous training treated as a public responsibility rather than an exclusively individual or firm-level concern. Fourth, universal healthcare and strong social insurance mean that job transitions carry less personal financial risk for Danish workers, reducing resistance to restructuring and facilitating mobility. input.how_sota_wins: Three compounding mechanisms, now measured directly from the winners' task-72 artifacts in /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/. (1) THE OPPONENT IS WEAK AND KNOWN: the 'reference' is April-2025 Gemini-2.5-pro Deep Research output, not human, and it is the weakest-insight report in the field. RACE is a single-prompt pairwise contrast (target always article_1), so beating 0.50 means out-writing one specific, year-old LLM house style in a side-by-side read. (2) STRUCTURE THAT MATCHES THE RUBRIC LITERALLY, ADAPTED TO GENRE: cellcog (SOTA family under our GPT-5.5 judge) writes task 72 as 11 H2 / 32 H3 with 178 single-idea paragraphs averaging 69 words — and ZERO bullets/tables on this task (it averages 48 table rows and 45 bullets corpus-wide; it suppresses them because task 72 is a literature review). The readability rubric names 'distinct heading levels' and 'single idea per paragraph' as criteria; tavily's 152-bullet fragment style is the field's worst readability (49.21). Because the judge writes a comparative analysis before scoring EVERY criterion, structural parity also stops the contrast-bleed that currently drags our insight and comprehensiveness. (3) A REPEATABLE FABRICATION-FREE SYNTHESIS PATTERN: cellcog's insight paragraphs are triads — grounded finding A (named study + number) + grounded finding B (conflicting named study) + a hedged reconciliation sentence carrying no new facts ('Both studies use rigorous methods... the divergence is therefore most plausibly attributable to institutional context rather than methodology'), then enumerated candidate explanations and an explicit 'current evidence cannot distinguish among these.' It institutionalizes this in a dedicated 'Critical Synthesis' section (What the Evidence Converges On / Outstanding Tensions / Limitations / Future Research). This is exactly the insight rubric's six criteria (causal mechanisms, second-order effects, challenging conventional wisdom, forward-looking) executed without a single unsourced factual claim. (4) COVERAGE VIA LENGTH DELIVERED IN MANY WRITING STEPS: cellcog's cleaned prose is ~11,200 words vs our 7,692 and the reference's ~7,700; qianfan (58.03 legacy) writes 47k words in 656 short paragraphs; WebWeaver's measured shape is ~25 discrete ~1k-token writing steps off a 4.9k-token outline. Sustained length with one-idea paragraphs is what moves comprehensiveness above parity. Caveat: onyx scores 54.54 (legacy) with only 4,554 words, so length is sufficient, not necessary — density of synthesis moves per section is the common factor.

transferable.technique: Genre-adaptive structural retrofit: 8-9 numbered H2 sections split into 25-32 H3 subsections; 60-150-word single-idea paragraphs with topic sentences; for task 72 specifically, academic-review prose register with FEW OR NO bullets (cellcog uses zero on this task; tavily's bullet-heavy style is punished). Keep the Wave2 comparison matrix — tables ARE rubric-named ('appropriate use of charts and tables') and qianfan/deepinsight use them — but as 1-3 purposeful tables, not decoration.
transferable.evidence: Measured from /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/cellcog.jsonl id=72 (32 H3, 178 paras @69w, 0 bullets/tables) vs tavily.jsonl id=72 (152 bullets, 15w paras, readability 49.21 = its worst dim); CONFIRMED readability rubric criteria in criteria_prompt_en.py; CONFIRMED ArticleCleaner preserves all structure.
transferable.maps_to_our_gap: Readability 0.3774 (worst-anomaly dimension, ~14 pts below the SOTA band) plus contrast-bleed into insight/comprehensiveness via the pairwise judge.
transferable.expected_points: Readability 0.377 -> 0.47-0.50 is +0.013-0.017 overall; contrast-bleed relief on other dims plausibly another +0.005-0.010.
transferable.risk: Low. Structure is judge-visible (CONFIRMED) and orthogonal to grounding. Only risk is bullet overuse — the evidence says restrain it for this genre.

transferable.technique: Synthesis triads + a dedicated 'Critical Synthesis' section: for each contested question, write grounded-finding-A + grounded-finding-B + a hedged reconciliation sentence that introduces NO new numbers/entities ('the divergence is most plausibly attributable to...'), then enumerate 2-3 candidate explanations and state explicitly what the evidence cannot yet distinguish. Add H2 with subsections: convergence / outstanding tensions / limitations of the literature / future research directions.
transferable.evidence: Measured verbatim from cellcog.jsonl id=72 sections 7.1 and 8.2 (Humlum-vs-Brynjolfsson reconciliation; three-tension enumeration); CONFIRMED insight rubric in criteria_prompt_en.py rewards exactly these sentence types; tavily's outline (section 7.1/7.2 consensus-vs-uncertainties) shows the same move; cellcog-max insight = 57.08, the field's best under our judge.
transferable.maps_to_our_gap: Insight 0.4238 at weight 0.32 — our worst dimension at the heaviest weight.
transferable.expected_points: Insight 0.424 -> 0.48-0.52 is +0.018-0.031 overall. This refines our Wave1 'interpretive weave': the deterministic bar on numbers/entities in inference sentences is validated — SOTA's reconciliation sentences carry no new facts — but the ADJACENT grounded sentences must name the studies being reconciled, so pair the weave with named-study attribution in running prose (our Wave2 'naming journals' item, upgraded to naming authors+years).
transferable.risk: None to faithfulness: the pattern is fabrication-free by construction. Risk is quality — a bad reconciliation sentence reads as filler; needs a rubric-scored revision pass.

transferable.technique: Multi-step section composition to expand cleaned-prose coverage from 7.7k to ~11k words: write each H3 in its own LLM call (~20-30 calls, WebWeaver's measured 24.78 writing steps) off a refined global outline, while keeping the full evidence corpus (not just section-scoped spans) available to the writer so cross-section reconciliation sentences remain possible.
transferable.evidence: CONFIRMED WebWeaver Table 3 measurements (25 writing steps, 26k output tokens, 4.9k-token outline, 2.2 outline optimization rounds); measured cellcog 11.2k cleaned prose words and qianfan 47k/59-H3/656-para shape; CONFIRMED warning from arXiv 2601.20843 + Tavily convergent design that section-siloed context kills cross-source synthesis — so sectioned WRITING, global CONTEXT.
transferable.maps_to_our_gap: Comprehensiveness 0.4549 (weight 0.29) and the structural impossibility of 24+ subsections in one monolithic pass.
transferable.expected_points: Comprehensiveness 0.455 -> 0.50 is +0.013; also carries instruction-following (+0.005-0.01) because literature-review conventions (scope/methodology section, future-directions section) come free with the sectioned outline.
transferable.risk: Cost/latency (20-30 calls; mind the <272K codex input cliff) and cross-section redundancy; mitigated by writing from a shared outline with per-section one-line summaries of what other sections already said.

transferable.technique: Rubric-targeted self-revision pass (TTD-DR-style, minimal version): after composition, run one LLM-judge critique against the six insight criteria + three readability criteria, revise once on the textual critique. Not the full evolutionary loop — one critique-revise cycle on the finished draft.
transferable.evidence: CONFIRMED TTD-DR self-evolution mechanism (fitness score + textual critique -> revise -> merge, arXiv 2507.16075); it is the only published mechanism for optimizing prose against a rubric, and our judge prompts are public (criteria_prompt_en.py).
transferable.maps_to_our_gap: Insight and readability jointly — converts 'average band' (4-6) sentences to 'good band' (6-8), which per the confirmed aggregation math is all parity requires.
transferable.expected_points: +0.005-0.015 overall, additive with the above; low-confidence because TTD-DR has no RACE score.
transferable.risk: Revision can dilute grounded claims; run the span-grounding verifier AFTER revision as a gate so the moat is enforced, not assumed.

not_transferable: The actual SOTA systems' internals: cellcog-max, qianfan, WhaleCloud, bodhi, lunon, dalpha are all closed with no papers — everything above is reverse-engineered from their output artifacts. We cannot copy their retrieval depth, backbone model, or any hidden revision machinery, and the last few points of their scores may live exactly there.

TTD-DR's core move — writing the initial draft from parametric knowledge and 'denoising' with retrieval — is a direct violation of span-grounded faithfulness (the draft IS ungrounded generation). Only its critique-revise loop is safe to take; the noisy-draft paradigm is not.

ADORE's memory-locked synthesis: it is a STRICTER section-scoped evidence constraint than we already have, would make our insight problem worse, and its headline benefit (traceability) is invisible to RACE anyway. Wrong system to imitate; confirmed.

qianfan's 47k-word brute-force length: 6x the reference, measured only under the legacy Gemini judge (never rescored under GPT-5.5), and would blow our token budget (codex input cost cliff at 272K). Length beyond ~11-12k cleaned words has no confirmed payoff under our judge.

Exploiting citation-invisibility as a strategy (dumping citation effort entirely): it is a RACE-v1-only quirk; DeepResearch Bench II reintroduces retrieval/InfoRecall scoring, and our faithfulness moat is the product story outside the benchmark. Keep citations; just know the judge cannot see them.

Tavily's architecture wholesale: its headline is token-cost engineering (66% reduction), it ranks 23rd, its bullet-heavy output style is measurably punished, and the widely-circulated 'Reflection Agent / Section Formatter' pipeline attributed to it is tutorial contamination, not Tavily.
input.verdict_on_our_gap: The 0.088 gap is doubly miscalibrated and the plan targets a phantom. (1) The reference is not human — it is April-2025 Gemini DR output, the weakest-insight report in the field, so 0.50 is a softer bar than we believed. (2) ADORE 0.5265 is not SOTA on any current board; under our pinned GPT-5.5 judge the real ladder is: parity 0.5000 at gap 0.0618, real SOTA cellcog-max 0.5578 at gap 0.1196. We currently sit between perplexity (0.4305) and openai-DR (0.4784). What the confirmed evidence explains: structural retrofit (+0.018-0.027), synthesis triads + critical-synthesis section (+0.018-0.031), multi-step composition for coverage (+0.018-0.023), one revision pass (+0.005-0.015) — realistic stacked total +0.05-0.08, landing 0.49-0.52. So the published record credibly explains getting to reference parity and slightly past it, i.e. roughly WhaleCloud/bodhi's neighborhood minus a couple of points. It does NOT explain the last ~0.04 to 0.5578: cellcog-max has no paper, its above-parity insight (0.5708, i.e. the judge scoring it ~1.33x the reference on the heaviest dimension) is only partially accounted for by the triad pattern I measured in its artifacts, and every published ablation with numbers (WebWeaver, TTD-DR) is either legacy-judge or non-RACE. Honest bottom line: our Wave1/Wave2 plan is directionally correct but under-specified in three ways the evidence fixes — restrain bullets for this genre (prose register, not brief register), add the named-study reconciliation triads and a dedicated tensions/convergence section rather than diffuse 'hedged inference sentences', and add the coverage/length lever (7.7k -> ~11k cleaned words via ~25 sectioned writing calls with global context) which the current plan omits entirely. Projected ceiling with all confirmed levers: ~0.51-0.52. Beyond that we would be reverse-engineering closed systems, and we should say so rather than promise 0.5578.
